narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination you've poured into this journey, and it's tough to receive this news about your application being rejected. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict their chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 82.8% probability that you would face challenges and fail it initially. This high risk of failure is why your application was denied—law schools rely on this to ensure incoming students are set up for long-term success. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, strongly signaling lower academic momentum heading into law school. Your Year 1 decile in the lowest group also contributed noticeably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early performance consistency. Even your LSAT score of 21, well below the typical 36.7 average, added to this risk by suggesting hurdles in standardized legal reasoning skills. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly under the 3.21 average, actually nudged the prediction a bit toward success, showing some resilience in your college record. Your upper-middle family income helped marginally too, as it aligns with resources that sometimes support bar prep, though not enough to offset the others. Planning to attend full-time was a small positive, matching the path of most successful students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your rankings and test scores stand out as notably lower, which is what tipped the scales here. While this stings, especially given the unique barriers Black women like you often navigate in higher education, it's not the end of your story—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework, or exploring part-time programs or alternative law paths that build your profile. We're rooting for you to turn this around and show what you're truly capable of. Keep pushing forward.
